food_subgroup: Egg-based dishes with seafood and starch binder

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Orh Luak or oyster omelette. 'Omelette food product' (FOODON_03302051) is used as the closest parent category. Truffle variant is not separately coded.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for oyster omelette or Orh Luak. 'Egg dishes' (A00LC) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Orh Luak, oyster omelette, or a sufficiently close Singapore hawker egg dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Truffle Orh Luak as served in a Singapore restaurant, approximately 280 g, comprising the egg-starch omelette base with oysters, truffle oil or shavings, and accompanying chilli sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Oyster Omelette (Orh Luak) — HPB Singapore Foodo / Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore Foodo provides nutrient data for standard Orh Luak (oyster omelette). The truffle variant (truffle oil or shavings added) is not separately listed. Values are adapted from the HPB standard Orh Luak entry with a modest upward adjustment to fat and energy to account for truffle oil addition, consistent with culinary practice. Core macros are anchored to the HPB Orh Luak reference. Secondary micronutrients not reported by HPB are set to -1.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Mixed Egg and Starch Dishes — analogous estimate based on sweet potato starch omelette
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Orh Luak or Truffle Orh Luak in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI is estimated at 52 based on the dominant carbohydrate source being sweet potato starch and tapioca starch, both of which have GI values in the range 46–55 per the University of Sydney GI Database (sweet potato GI ~44–54; tapioca starch ~67 but diluted by egg protein matrix which lowers effective GI). A blended estimate of 52 is used, consistent with mixed starch-egg dishes. GL per serving = (52 × 14.8 × 2.80) / 100 = 21.6 g. gi_category is 'low' per the ≤55 threshold rule.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g; ~1176 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid Truffle Orh Luak due to its high sodium content from oyster sauce, chilli sauce, and seasoning; a single serving may contribute more than 50% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load from sweet potato and tapioca starch binder with low-to-moderate GI (~52)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes can consume Truffle Orh Luak occasionally in controlled portions, as the starch binder contributes a moderate glycemic load (~21.6 g GL per serving); pairing with non-starchy vegetables and monitoring portion size is advised.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated saturated fat from eggs and cooking oil, combined with high sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should consume Truffle Orh Luak in moderation, as the combination of saturated fat (~3.2 g per 100 g) and high sodium (~420 mg per 100 g) may adversely affect blood pressure and lipid profiles; frequency should be limited to occasional consumption.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Truffle Orh Luak is a premium Singaporean hawker dish that elevates the classic Orh Luak (oyster omelette) with the addition of truffle oil or fresh truffle shavings. The base dish consists of fresh oysters and eggs bound together with a sweet potato starch and tapioca starch slurry, pan-fried in lard or oil until the edges are crispy while the centre remains soft and custardy. The truffle component adds an earthy, umami-rich aroma that complements the briny sweetness of the oysters. The dish is typically served with a tangy-spicy chilli sauce on the side. Nutritionally, Truffle Orh Luak is a moderate-energy dish (~175 kcal per 100 g) with meaningful protein from eggs and oysters (~7.2 g per 100 g), moderate fat (~10.5 g per 100 g) including saturated fat from cooking oil and eggs, and a starch-based carbohydrate profile (~14.8 g per 100 g) with a low-to-moderate glycemic index (~52). Sodium content is high (~420 mg per 100 g), making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is not suitable for shellfish-allergic individuals. Culturally, Orh Luak is a beloved Teochew-origin dish deeply embedded in Singapore hawker culture, and the truffle variant represents a modern premium interpretation served in casual-dining and food-court settings. It is typically classified as a mixed egg-seafood dish and is not vegetarian, vegan, or halal-certified in most preparations due to the use of lard and shellfish.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Truffle Orh Luak?
Is Orh Luak high in sodium?
What is the GI of Orh Luak?
What are the ingredients in Orh Luak?
What is the difference between Orh Luak and Orh Ah Jian?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from the HPB Singapore Foodo standard Orh Luak entry, which is an approved primary source for Singapore dishes. The truffle variant is not separately listed in HPB Foodo; fat and energy values have been modestly adjusted upward to account for truffle oil addition. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database based on the starch composition. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo standard Orh Luak data. The truffle variant adds truffle oil or shavings, which increases fat and energy slightly; this adaptation is documented in nutrient_source. 2. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Orh Luak or Truffle Orh Luak was found in the University of Sydney GI Database as of the access date. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source for this dish. polyunsaturated_fat_g is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source. trans_fat_g is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source. potassium_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source. calcium_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source. iron_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source. cholesterol_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source. vitamin_c_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source. vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source. vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source. vitamin_b3_niacin_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source. vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source. vitamin_b12_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source. phosphorus_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source. magnesium_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source. zinc_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source. water_g is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source. 4. The dish is not halal-certified in most preparations due to the use of lard; individual restaurant practices may vary. 5. The input cuisine tag 'Global' has been corrected to 'Singaporean' as Orh Luak is a Teochew-origin Singapore hawker dish. 6. food_group has been corrected from 'Grains and staples' to 'Mixed' as this is a composite egg-seafood-starch dish.
